Facts.text: Council of Chalcedon (4th ecumenical council) opens - acts of second Council of Ephesus annulled and doctrine of Pope Leo's Tome adopted
Battle at Andernach: East Frankish king Louis the Younger heavily defeats the West Frankish king Charles the Bald
Dmitar Zvonimir is crowned king of Croatia
San Marcos ministry in Venice initiated
San Marino adopts its constitution
Supernova "Kepler's nova" first sighted by Lodovico delle Colombe in Italy
Massachusetts Bay Colony forms its first government
Siege of Belgrade, Ottoman troops capture Belgrade
French privateer Jacques Cassard seen on Suriname coast
Chinese assault on Diestpoort, Batavia
British forces lay siege to French port of Boulogne using Congreve rockets, invented by Sir William Congreve
Treaty of Ried between Bavaria & Austria
Jack Broughton designed padded gloves first used in a competitive boxing match between two unnamed English boxers at Aix-la-Chapelle in France
First eruption of Galunggung in Java sends boiling sludge into valley
"Ke Kumukānāwai a me nā Kānāwai o ko Hawaiʻi Pae ʻĀina, Honolulu, 1840", the 1st written Constitution of the Hawaiian Kingdom enacted by King Kamehameha III and Kuhina Nui (Prime Minister) Kekāuluohi
First Hawaiian constitution proclaimed
Second Opium War or Second Anglo-Chinese War begins with the Arrow Incident on the Pearl River
Telegraph line between LA & San Francisco opens
Battle of Perryville, Kentucky - Confederate invasion halted
Earthquake in Santa Cruz Mountains
Forest fire destroys Peshtigo, Wisconsin, killing between 1,200 and 2,500 people, making it then the deadliest wildfire in recorded history
First women's prison run by women opens at Indiana Reformatory Institute
Battle of Angamos, War of the Pacific: Chilean Navy defeats the Peruvian Navy and kills Peruvian Admiral Miguel Grau
Phillies set club record 16th straight victory
Ohio Valley Improvement Association forms
First Canadian Intercollegiate football game: McGill beats Queen's, 3-2
J M Synge's play "In the Shadow of the Glen" premieres in Dublin
Vanderbilt Cup, first major trophy in American auto racing is conducted on public roads in Nassau County area of Long Island, NY; inaugural winner, American driver George Heath in a Panhard
Karl Nessler demonstrates first 'permanent wave' for hair in London
Chicago Cubs beat NY Giants 4-2 in a playoff to win NL pennant
NY Giants set season attendance record at 910,000 (broken in 1920)
Montenegro declares war on Turkey, beginning 1st Balkan War
Battle of Loos ends as German forces contain the British attack, resulting in 85,000 casualties
Joseph Szigeti debuts Alfredo Casella's Violin Concerto
Mohammed Nadir Khan occupies Kabul Afghanistan and drives out Habibullah Ghazi
The Indian Air Force is established
Coit Tower dedicated in San Francisco as a monument to firefighters
Martinez Barrios forms new Spanish government
Germany annexes Western Poland
German troops occupies Romania
Building at Concentration Camp Birkenau begins
Fight at Matanikau, Guadalcanal (John Hersey-Into the Valley)
Great Britain establishes bases on Azores
"The Adventures of Ozzie and Harriet" debut on CBS radio
Microwave oven patented by US inventor Percy Spencer
Military plane crashes at Christian HBS, 24 die
Cleveland Browns play Pittsburgh for 1st time, beat Steelers 30-17
112 people killed in Britain's worst peace-time rail crash at Harrow and Wealdstone, northwest London
Chinese offensive in Korea
WTAP TV channel 15 in Parkersburg-Marietta, WV (NBC) begins
World's most powerful aircraft carrier, USS Saratoga, launched
Brooklyn Dodgers announce move to Los Angeles
Procter & Gamble director N McElroy becomes US Secretary of Defense
Soviet spy Jack Sobel sentenced to 7 years (NYC)
Turkish & Syrian border guards exchange fire
Dr Ake Senning installs first pacemaker (Stockholm)
KCMT TV channel 7 in Alexandria, MN (CBS/NBC/ABC) begins broadcasting
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site

American Phil Hill's Ferrari team does not participate in season ending US Grand Prix at Watkins Glen having already clinched World Drivers Championship; English driver Innes Ireland wins Lotus' first ever Grand Prix
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya, USSR
Algeria admitted as 109th member of UN
North Korea reports 100% election turnout, miraculously 100% vote for the Workers' Party

Spiegel scandal: Der Spiegel publishes article "Bedingt abwehrbereit" ("Conditionally prepared for defense") about NATO manoeuver called "Fallex 62", uncovering sorry state of West German army. Magazine soon accused of treason.
Sultan of Zanzibar cedes his mainland possessions to Kenya
Gilroy Roberts becomes first US chief engraver to retire (than die)
Ringo Starrpasses his driving test
Muslims in Jakarta set fire to PKI headquarters
Post Office Tower opens in London, then the tallest building in England
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Wyoming's Jerry DePoyster kicks 3 field goals over 50 yds (54, 54, 52)
Dutch aircraft carrier Karel Doorman (formerly British HMS Venerable) sold to Argentina
The opening rally of the Days of Rage occurs, organized by the Weather Underground in Chicago, Illinois.
The Social Democratic and Labour Party (SDLP) propose that a system of Proportional Representation (PR) should be used in elections in Northern Ireland
John Lennonreleases his megahit single "Imagine"
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Harold Carmichael begins NFL streak of 127 consecutive game receptions
International music revue "From Israel with Love" closes limited run at Palace Theater, NYC, after 8 performances
OPEC meets with oil companies to discuss revision of 1971 Tehran agreement and oil prices; negotiations fail.
Spyris Markezinis forms government in Greece
Franklin National Bank collapses due to fraud and mismanagement; at the time it was the largest bank failure in the history of the United States.
San Antonio Spurs, then members of the ABA, defeat the NBA's Atlanta Hawks, 109-107 in the first basketball game ever played in the Louisiana Superdome
Largest baseball crowd in Penns, 64,924 see Dodgers beat Phillies 4-1 in 4th NL championship game (Dodgers win pennant)
Ken Warby sets world water speed record at 275.97 knots (511.10 km/h; 317.58 mph) on Blowering Dam, Tumut River NSW, Australia
Yanks win 3rd straight AL Championship, all against Kansas City
British Leyland starts selling Mini Metro
USSR & Syria sign peace treaty
USSR performs nuclear test
New Jersey Devils 1st NHL victory; beat New York Rangers 3-2 at Meadowlands
Poland bans Solidarity & all labor unions
1st regular season Islander OT game beat Caps 8-7
Washington Capitals 1st NHL overtime game losing to NY Islanders 8-7
NBC's premiere of TV made "The Burning Bed", based on Francine Hughes
"Rembrandt & Hitler or Me" premieres in Amsterdam
Alain Boubil and Herbert Kretzner's musical "Les Misérables" premieres in London at the Barbican Centre
Little Richardseriously injured in a single car accident in Los Angeles
Mike Scott ties playoff record of 14 strikeouts, beats Mets 1-0
Run-DMC calls for a day of peace among LA street gangs
Fire in the Space Needle causes evacuation and results in damages of $2,000 in Seattle, Washington
Jay Howell ejected in NLCS game 3 for having pine tar on his glove
Israeli police kill 17 Palestinian rioters
US doctors Joseph E. Murray & E. Donnall Thomas win Nobel Prize for Physiology or Medicine for bone marrow transport to treat cancer and blood diseases
Discovery of a African-American cemetery in Manhattan announced - largest and earliest burial ground of free and enslaved Africans in America, later a National Monument
Nobel Prize for literature is given to West Indies poet Derek Walcott
Ottawa Senators 1st NHL game
Pioneer Venus Orbiter (1st Venus orbiter-1978), crashes into Venus
Video game Mortal Kombat first released as a 2D fighting game by Midway Games
UN lifts remaining economic sanctions against South Africa
BPAA US Women's Bowling Open won by Aleta Sill
Edgar Martinez drives home the tying and winning runs to rally the Mariners to a 6-5 win in the bottom of the 11th, beating the Yankees to win the AL Division Series
Oslo's Gardermoen Airport opens after the closure of Fornebu
Beginning of a new era of the Coligny calendar, the oldest known material Celtic calendar
Musical "Peter Pan" starring Cathy Rigby, and filmed before a live audience, premieres on A&E TV
A twin-engine Cessna and a Scandinavian Airlines System McDonnell Douglas MD-87 collide in heavy fog during takeoff from Linate Airport in Milan, Italy, killing 118 people
Magnitude 7.6 earthquake in Azad Jammu and Kashmir, Pakistan, kills an estimated 86,000 people across Afghanistan, India and Pakistan in the deadliest earthquake in South Asian history
Nobel Prize for Medicine or Physiology awarded jointly to Harald zur Hausen (cause of cervical cancer) and Françoise Barré-Sinoussi, Luc Montagnier (discovery of HIV)
Irish professional darts player Brendan Dolan plays 1st perfect 9 dart game on TV in semi-final against James Wade at PDC World Darts Championship in Dublin
35 people are killed by a Nigerian military bomb struck a convoy in Maiduguri
Mustafa A.G. Abushagur, the first elected Libyan Prime Minister, is voted out of office by the Libyan parliament
Peter Higgs and François Englert win the 2013 Nobel Prize in Physics for their work on the origin of the mass of subatomic particles
Eric Betzig, Stefan Hell and William Moerner win the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for the development of super-resolved fluorescence microscopy
The Vienna Philharmonic Orchestra is awarded the third $1 million Birgit Nilsson Prize
"Once Upon a Time in Shaolin" hip hop album by Wu-Tang released - the most expensive piece of music ever with just one physical two-CD copy, auctioned for about $2 million
Nobel prize for Literature awarded to Belarusian journalist and author Svetlana Alexievich
140 people are killed in an air strike during a wake in Sanaa, Yemen
New Zealand routs South Africa, 57-14 in Durban to win their 4th Rugby Championship; undefeated All Blacks score 9 tries to 1, 2 each to Israel Dagg, TJ Perenara and Beauden Barrett
Wildfires ignite in Northern Californian wine country, killing at least 41 over the next week, with 20,000 evacutated
Major climate report by Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change for the UN says planet will warm 2.7 degrees Fahrenheit (1.5 degrees Celsius) by 2040 with dire results
Nobel prize for Economics awarded to William Nordhaus for climate change and Paul Romer for endogenous growth theory
Red Sox utility Brock Holt becomes first MLB player to hit for the cycle in a postseason game during Boston's 16-1 Game 3 rout of the NY Yankees in the AL Division Series at Yankee Stadium
FBI confirm Samuel Little is America's most prolific serial killer, after verifying more than half of his 93 confessed murders
Montgomery, Alabama, home of the US civil rights movement, elects Steven Reed as its first black mayor in 200 years
Noble Prize for Physics awarded to James Peebles (work on evolution of the universe), and Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz (discovery of planet 51 Pegasi b, orbiting another star)
American poet Louise Glück is awarded the Nobel Prize for Literature
FBI charge 13 men with plotting to kidnap Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer and storm the Michigan Capitol
Landslide caused by unusually heavy rain kills at least 50 in Las Tejerias, Venezuela
The Kerch bridge built by Russia to link Crimea to Russia and symbol of Russia's illegal occupation of Ukraine is partly blown up, allowing only light traffic to cross
Israel formally declares war on Hamas as the death toll reaches about 1,100 on both sides and Israel increases airstrikes on Gaza

South Australia cricket batsman Jake Fraser-McGurk breaks AB de Villiers' 2015 record for the fastest century in a List A game, plundering a 29-ball hundred against Tasmania in Adelaide; out on 125 from 38 balls
Battle at Cibalae: Roman emperor in the westConstantine the Greatdefeats eastern Emperor Licinius at Cibalae (modern Croatia) date disputed
Duke of Bavaria Maximilian I and Holy Roman EmperorFerdinand IIsign the Treaty of Munich
English AdmiralGeorge Villiers' fleet sails from Plymouth to Cadiz, Spain
First hospital in Montreal, Quebec founded, the Hôtel-Dieu de Montréal by nurseJeanne Mance

CaptainJames Cooklands in New Zealand for the first time, near present-day Gisborne on the East Coast of the North Island. A misunderstanding, possibly over a ceremonial challenge causes the English to shoot and kill Ngāti Oneone leader, Te Maro.
Officers decide to bar slaves and free blacks from the Continental Army
English engraver and poetWilliam Blakebegins study at the Royal Academy, Old Somerset House, London
Joachim Murat's French forces land at Pizzo, Italy
The government of GeneralJosé de San Martínestablishes the Peruvian Navy
Sergei Rachmaninofffirst performs "Prelude in C-sharp-Minor" in Moscow
EmperorFranz Joseph Inames composerGustav Mahleras Director of the Vienna Court Opera
Leon Trotskyis named chairman of the Petrograd Soviet as Bolsheviks gain control
British Labour government ofRamsay MacDonaldfalls to Conservatives
"The Second Hundred Years" silent short film is released, starringStan LaurelandOliver Hardy, the first Laurel and Hardy film with them appearing as a team
Bruno Hauptmann is indicted for the murder ofCharles Lindbergh's son
George S. Kaufman &Moss Hart's play "Fabulous Invalid" premieres in NYC
Comedy duo Abbott andCostellolaunch their weekly radio show
Samuel Barber's "Capricorn Concerto" premieres at The Town Hall, NYC, in a performance by Daniel Saidenberg and his Little Symphony
Birmingham, Alabama, barsJackie Robinson's Negro-White All-Stars from playing there Robinson gives in & drops white players from his group
Conservatives led byHarold Macmillanwin the British general election
Guerrilla leaderChe Guevaraand his men are captured in Bolivia
Soviet authorAleksandr Solzhenitsynwins the Nobel Prize for Literature
Apple Records releasesJohn Lennon's second solo studio album, "Imagine" in UK; it tops the charts in US, UK, Australia, and 3 other countries

Despite retiring with suspension trouble at the US Grand Prix at Watkins Glen,Emerson Fittipaldibecomes first Brazilian to win the Formula 1 World Drivers Championship; wins by 16 points fromJackie Stewart
MLB Detroit Tiger pitcher Lerrin LaGrow and Oakland A's shortstopBert Campaneris, each fined and suspended when Campaneris flung his bat at the mound after getting hit by a pitch
MLB NLCS game 3 interrupted by brawl spawned by fight between Cincinnati RedsPete Roseand New York Mets Bud Harrelson at Shea Stadium in New York
EMI signs British punk rock band "Sex Pistols"

AmericanMario Andrettiin a Lotus finishes 10th in the season ending Canadian Grand Prix at Île Notre-Dame Circuit, but wins his first Formula 1 World Drivers Championship by 13 points from Ronnie Peterson
US PresidentRonald Reagangreets predecessorsJimmy Carter,Gerald FordandRichard Nixonbefore sending them to Egypt forAnwar Sadat's funeral
MGM releases comedy film "My Favorite Year", directed by Richard Benjamin, and starringPeter O'Toole, Mark Linn-Baker, Jessica Harper, and Joseph Bologna
"The Color of Money", directed byMartin Scorseseand starringPaul NewmanandTom Cruise, premieres in New York City
Finishing 741-week stay,Pink Floyd's album "The Dark Side of the Moon" makes final appearance (of initial run) on Billboard 200 Albums chart
Croatian Parliament cuts all remaining ties with Yugoslavia
Howard Sternreleases his 1st book "Private Parts"
Dolphin'sDan Marinobreaks Tarkenton's NFL career completions record
José Saramagois the first person from Portugal to be awarded the Nobel Prize for Literature
German Ferrari driverMichael Schumacherwins Japanese Grand Prix at Suzuka to clinch his 3rd, and 1st of 5 straight F1 World Drivers Championships
US PresidentGeorge W. Bushannounces the establishment of the Office of Homeland Security
Clive GrangerandRobert F. Engleawarded Nobel Prize in Economic Sciences “for methods of analyzing economic time series with time-varying volatility (ARCH)”
Peter Agreand Roderick MacKinnon win the 2003 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for discoveries concerning channels in cell membranes
KenyanWangari Maathaibecomes the first African woman to receive the Nobel Peace Prize for "her contribution to sustainable development, democracy, and peace"
Martha Stewartbegins 5 months incarcerated at Federal Prison Camp, Alderson for insider trading and obstruction of justice
NHL greatWayne Gretzkygets his first win as a coach as the Phoenix Coyotes beat the Minnesota Wild 2-1 at Jobing.com Arena, Phoenix
Film "Lincoln" directed bySteven Spielberg, starringDaniel Day-Lewis, base on the biography byDoris Kearns Goodwinpremieres
Hugo Chávezis re-elected as president of Venezuela for a fourth term
John B. GurdonandShinya Yamanakawin the 2012 Nobel Prize for Medicine for their work on reprogramming end stage cells to become pluripotent
American actress and singerSelena Gomezreveals she has Lupus

New Orleans quarterbackDrew Breesbecomes NFL's all-time leader in passing yards; needs 201 yards to pass Peyton Manning's record; gets 363 & 3 TD's in Saints 43-19 win v Washington Redskins at the SuperDome
Nobel Peace Prize awarded to journalistsMaria Ressaof the Philippines andDmitry Muratovof Russia "for their efforts to safeguard freedom of expression"
Dutch driverMax Verstappenscores his 14th win of the F1 season with a dominant victory in the Qatar Grand Prix at the Lusail Circuit; clinches his third consecutive Formula 1 title
Noble Prize for Physics awarded toJohn HopfieldandGeoffrey Hinton"for foundational discoveries and inventions that enable machine learning with artificial neural networks"

Great Stand on the Ugra river: Standoff between forces of Akhmat Khan, Khan of the Great Horde andIvan III, Grand Prince of all Rus. Ends with a Tatar-Mongol retreat, leading to the disintegration of the Horde.
The Great Chicago Fire kills an estimated 300 people and destroys over 4 square miles (10 square km) of buildings and the original Emancipation Proclamation
American soldier Alvin York single-handedly attacks German gun nest, killing at least 25 and capturing 132 Germans
US House of Representatives votes to begin impeachment hearings against PresidentBill Clintonon charges of lying about his affair with Monica Lewinsky
Margaret Douglas, Countess of Lennox, born in Harbottle Castle, Northumberland, England (d. 1578)
Giulio Caccini, Italian composer, violist, tenor, and teacher, born in Florence, Republic of Florence (d. 1610)
Philipp von Zesen, German poet and historian (Bescheibung of Amsterdam), born in Dessau, Germany (d. 1689)
Maximilian Henry of Bayern, Archbishop-Elector of Cologne, Bishop of Hildesheim and Bishop of Liège, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1688)
Benito Jerónimo Feijóo y Montenegro, Spanish scholar, born in Casdemiro, Spain (d. 1764)
Jaime de Casellas, Spanish composer, born in Valls, Spain (d. 1764)
Antonio Palella, Italian composer and harpsichordist, born in San Giovanni a Teduccio, Naples, Habsburg Spain (d. 1761)
Cornelis Troost, Dutch portrait painter (Unseemly Love), born in Amsterdam (d. 1750)
Yechezkel Landau, Polish rabbi and Talmudist, born in Opatów, Poland (d. 1793)
Michel Benoist, French Jesuit missionary and scientist, born in Dijon, France (d. 1774)
Jonathan Mayhew, American Congregational minister, born in Martha's Vineyard, Massachusetts (d. 1766)
Michel-Julien Mathieu, French composer, born in Fontainebleau, France (d. 1777)
Jean-François Rewbell, French lawyer and politician, born in Colmar, France (d. 1807)
Johann Wilhelm Stadler, German composer (Die Kreuzfahrer - The Crusaders), born in Repperndorf, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1819)
Franz Seydelmann, German composer, born in Dresden, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1806)
Harman Blennerhassett, Irish lawyer, born in Hampshire, England (d. 1831)
Arvid August Afzelius, Swedish poet and historian, born in Fjällåkra, Sweden (d. 1871)
John Ruggles, American politician, born in Westborough, Massachusetts (d. 1874)
William Swainson, English naturalist, ornithologist and natural history artist, born in Newington, London (d. 1855)
Waldemar Thrane, Norwegian violinist, composer (Fjeldeventyret), and conductor, born in Oslo, Norway (d. 1828)
Eeltsje Hiddes Halbertsma, Dutch Frisian writer, poet and physician, born in Grou, Frisia, Batavian Republic (d. 1858)
Peter Hofstede de Groot, Dutch reformed theologist, born in Leer, Germany (d. 1886)
Carl Ludwig Amand Mangold, German composer, born in Darmstadt, Grand Duchy of Hesse, Confederation of the Rhine (d. 1889)
John Henninger Reagan, American Confederate politician, born in Sevier County, Tennessee (d. 1905)
Matt Whitaker Ransom, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Warren County, North Carolina (d. 1904)
Francisque Sarcey, French writer (Le Temps), born in Dourdan, France (d. 1899)
Walter Kittredge, American musician during American Civil War, born in Merrimack, New Hampshire (d. 1905)
Otto Winter-Hjelm, Norwegian musician and composer, born in Oslo, Norway (d. 1931)
Rose Scott, Australian social reformer and suffragette, born in Glendon, New South Wales, Australia (d. 1925)
Henri Louis le Chatelier, French chemist, born in Paris (d. 1936)
Gustav Ehrismann, German author and expert on the German language, born in Pforzheim, Baden-Württemberg (d. 1941)
Felix Woyrsch, German composer, born in Opava, Czech Republic (d. 1944)
Theodore Roberts, American actor (Roaring Road, 10 Commandments), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1928)
Edythe Chapman, American actress (Beyond the Rocks), born in Rochester, New York (d. 1948)
Ozias Leduc, Canadian painter, born in Saint-Hilaire-de-Rouville, Quebec (d. 1955)
Max Slevogt, German painter, born in Landshut, Germany (d. 1932)
J. Frank Duryea, American inventor (Duryea Motor Wagon Company, first auto built in the US), born in Washburn, Illinois (d. 1967)
Komitas [Soghomon Soghomonian], Armenian priest, composer (Patarag), and musicologist, born in Kütahya, Ottoman Empire (now Turkey) (d. 1935) [O.S. birth 09-26]
Louis Vierne, French organist (Notre-Dame, 1900-37), and composer (Messe solennelle), born in Poitiers, France (d. 1937)
Albert Knight, English cricket batsman (3 Tests, 1 x 50; Leicestershire CCC), born in Leicester, England (d. 1946)
John Cowper Powys, British writer (Wood & Stone, Wolf Solent), born in Shirley, Derbyshire (d. 1963)
Ejnar Hertzsprung, Danish astronomer (Hertzsprung-Russell diagram), born in Frederiksberg, Denmark (d. 1967)
Nance O'Neil, American actor (Cimarron), born in Oakland, California (d. 1965)
Willie Smith, Scottish golfer (US Open 1899), born in Dundee, Scotland (d. 1916)
Hans Heysen, German-born landscape artist, born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1968)

Chen Duxiu, Chinese revolutionary socialist and author who co-founded the Chinese Communist Party with Li Dazhao and was the 1st General Secretary (1921-27), born in Anqing, Anhui, Qing dynasty of China (d. 1927)
Ernest Douwes Dekker, Indo-Dutch nationalist and founder of National-India Party, born in Pasuruan, Malang, Indonesia (d. 1950)
Dick Burnett, American musician ("Man of Constant Sorrow"), born in Monticello, Kentucky (d. 1977)
Otto Heinrich Warburg, German physician (Nobel-1931), born in Freiburg, Baden, Germany (d. 1970)
Walther von Reichenau, German field marshal (The Severity Order - allowing for killing non-combatant Jews), born in Karlsruhe, Germany (d. 1942)
Johnny Lush, American MLB baseball pitcher, 1904-10 (Philadelphia Phillies, St . Louis Cardinals 2 no-hitters - 1 official, 1 rain-shortened), born in Williamsport, Pennsylvania (d. 1946)

Will Vodery, African-American theater and film arranger, orchestrator (Showboat; Ziegfeld's Follies; Blackbirds), and composer (From Dixie to Broadway), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1951)[1]
Huntley Gordon, Canadian actor, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1956)
Clifford Heatherley, British actor (Who Killed Doc Robin?), born in Preston, Lancashire, England (d. 1937)
Collett E. Woolman, American airline founder (Delta Air Service), born in Bloomington, Indiana (d. 1966)
Philippe Thys, Belgian road cyclist (Tour de France 1913, 14, 20; 13 x individual stages), born in Anderlecht, Belgium (d. 1971)
Eddie Rickenbacker, American aviator "Ace of Aces" (WWI), born in Columbus, Ohio (d. 1973)
Snuffy Browne, West Indian cricket all-rounder (4 Tests, 1 x 50, 6 wickets; British Guiana, Barbados), born in St. Michael, Barbados (d. 1973)
Orovida Camille Pissarro, British painter and etcher, born in Epping, England (d. 1968)
Jeanne Gabrielle Willing, Dutch author (Sinner & the Girl), born in Amsterdam (d. 1984)
Zog I, King of Albania (1928-39), born in Burgajet Castle, Ottoman Empire (d. 1961)
Julien Duvivier, French film director (La Bandera), born in Lille, France (d. 1967)
Rouben Mamoulian, American movie director and author (The Mark of Zorro, Applause), born in Tbilisi, Georgia (d. 1987)
Clarence Williams, American composer, born in Plaquemine, Louisiana (d. 1965)
Milner Gray, British graphic designer, born in London (d. 1997)
Geoffrey Jellicoe, English landscape architect (Shute House), born in London (d. 1996)
Serge Chermayeff, Russian-British architect and designer, born in Grozny, Russia (d. 1996)
Zeno Vancea, Romanian composer, born in Bocsa, Romania (d. 1990)
Eivind Groven, Norwegian composer and ethnomusicologist, born in Eidsborg, Norway (d. 1977)
Lina Radke, German 800m runner (Olympic gold 1928), born in Karlsruhe, Germany (d. 1983)
René Guillou, French organist and composer (L'Autre mère), born in Rennes, Brittany, France (d. 1958)
Howard Joslin, American actor (Quebec, Detective Story), born in Georgia (d. 1975)
Paul Van Buskirk Yoder, American composer, born in Tacoma, Washington (d. 1990)
Bill Hewitt, American Pro Football HOF end (4 × First-team All-Pro; Chicago Bears, Philadelphia Eagles; one last players to not wear a helmet), born in Bay City, Michigan (d. 1947)
Gus Hall, American union organizer and head of the U.S. Communist Party, born in Cherry Township, Minnesota (d. 2000)
Kirk Alyn [John Feggo Jr], American actor (Superman), born in Oxford, New Jersey (d. 1999)
Robert Gilruth, American aviation and space pioneer (NASA Director of the Manned Spacecraft Center, 1961-74), born in Nashwauk, Minnesota (d. 2000)[1]
Walter Schumann, American choral director (Ford Show) and film and television composer (Night Of The Hunter; Dragnet theme), born in New York City (d. 1958)
William A. Egan, American Democratic politician, 1st Governor of Alaska (1959-66, 1970-74), born in Valdez, Territory of Alaska (d. 1984)
Spark Matsunaga, United States Senator from Hawaii (1977-90), born in Kukuiula, Hawaii (d. 1990)
Danny Murtaugh, American baseball infielder (NL stolen base leader 1941 Philadelphia Phillies) and manager (World Series 1960, 71; Pittsburgh Pirates), born in Chester, Pennsylvania (d. 1976)
Hans Poser, German composer, born in Tannenbergsthal, Saxony, Germany (d. 1970)
Walter Lord, American author (A Night to Remember), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 2002)
Jens Christian Skou, Danish scientist (Nobel Prize for Chemistry 1997), born in Lemvig, Denmark (d. 2018)
Ron Randell, Australian actor (Loves of Carmen, I am a Camera), born in Sydney, New South Wales (d. 2005)
Hal Singer, American jazz and R&B saxophonist ("Corn Bread"), born in Tulsa, Oklahoma (d. 2020)[1]
Kiichi Miyazawa, 78th Prime Minister of Japan (1991-93), born in Tokyo (d. 2007)
Mac Anderson, New Zealand cricketer (played in 1st NZ-Aust Test 1946), born in Westport, West Coast, New Zealand (d. 1979)
Maxi Herber, German figure skater pairs (Olympic gold 1936), born in Munich, Germany (d. 2006)
Abraham Sarmiento, Sr., Filipino Supreme Court jurist (1987-91), born in Santa Cruz, Ilocos Sur, Philippine (d. 2010)
Douglas Gray, Scottish record and film producer, born in Aberdeen (d. 1997)
Svend Westergaard, Danish composer, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1988)
Alphons Egli, Swiss politcian and member of the Swiss Federal Council, born in Lucerne, Switzerland (d. 2016)
Arkady Vorobyev, Russian middle heavyweight (Olympic gold 1956, 60), born in Tetyushi, Republic of Tatarstan, Russia (d. 2012)
Andrei Donatovich Sinyavsky, Russian writer and critic, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1997)
Raaj Kumar, Hindi film star (Mother India), born in Loralai, Balochistan, British India (d. 1996)
(Georgeanne) "Gigi" Durston, American cabaret and big band singer (Sonny Kendis Show; Russ Case Orchestra), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1988)
Al Duncan, American blues drummer, born in McKinney, Texas (d. 1995)
César Milstein, Argentine biochemist (Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1984), born in Bahía Blanca, Argentina (d. 2002)
Jim Elliot, American Christian missionary, born in Portland, Oregon (d. 1956)
Raymond Chow, Hong Kong film producer, popularized martial arts worldwide, born in Hong Kong (d. 2018)
Torbjorn Falkanger, Norwegian ski jumper who took the Olympic oath in 1952, born in Trondheim, Norway (d. 2013)
Bill Maynard [Walter Williams], English actor (Greengrass-Heartbeat) and comedian, born in Heath End, England (d. 2018)
M. Russell Ballard, American religious leader (Acting President of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles of LDS Church. 2018-23), born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 2023)
Neil Harvey, Australian cricket batsman (79 Tests @ 48.41, 21 x 100, HS 205; Victoria CA, NSW CA), born in Melbourne, Australia
Betty Boothroyd, English politician (1st female Speaker of the UK House of Commons 1992-2000), born in Dewsbury, England (d. 2023)
Didi (Waldyr Pereira), Brazilian soccer midfielder (68 caps; Fluminense), born in Campos dos Goytacazes, Brazil (d. 2001)
(Park) "Pepper" Adams, American jazz saxophonist (Thad Jones/Mel Lewis Big Band), born in Highland Park, Michigan (d. 1986)

Faith Ringgold (née Jones), American mixed-media artist (Who's Afraid of Aunt Jemima?), children's literature author and illustrator (We Came to America), and educator, born in Harlem, New York City (d. 2024)[1]
